glossary.def: A model that learns p(x | y) and p(y) — a full account of how each class produces its data — then applies Bayes' rule to recover p(y | x). It can be sampled from to synthesize new examples. Naive Bayes, LDA/QDA and HMMs are generative.

A model that learns the boundary p(y | x) directly and never models how the inputs are generated. Logistic regression, SVMs and neural nets are discriminative; they usually win with abundant data, generative models with scarce data.

The defining shortcut of naive Bayes: features are assumed mutually independent given the class, so the joint likelihood factors as p(x | y) = ∏_j p(x_j | y). This collapses an exponential number of parameters to O(d) per class. Almost never literally true, yet the basis of the whole method.

p(x | y): how plausible the observed features are under each class's generative story. In Gaussian NB it is a product of per-feature normals; in multinomial NB a product of word probabilities. The classifier multiplies it by the prior and picks the argmax.

Adding a pseudo-count α to every category so no estimated probability is ever zero: p(w | y) = (N_{y,w} + α) / (N_y + αV). It cures the zero-frequency problem (one unseen word vetoing a class) and is equivalent to a Dirichlet prior; α = 1 is Laplace, α = 0.5 is Jeffreys.

When a feature value never co-occurs with a class in training, its unsmoothed conditional probability is 0, which drives the entire product likelihood to zero and unconditionally vetoes that class. Smoothing exists to keep every probability strictly positive.

The continuous-feature variant: each per-feature likelihood is a 1-D normal whose mean and variance are estimated per class. Its per-class density is an axis-aligned Gaussian, making it exactly Quadratic Discriminant Analysis with off-diagonal covariances forced to zero.

How well a model's stated confidence matches empirical frequency. Naive Bayes is famously well-classified but poorly calibrated — correlated features make it double-count evidence and push posteriors toward 0 or 1 — so Platt scaling or isotonic regression is needed when calibrated probabilities matter.

refs.cite: Domingos, P. & Pazzani, M. (1997). On the Optimality of the Simple Bayesian Classifier under Zero-One Loss. Machine Learning 29.
Maron, M. E. (1961). Automatic Indexing: An Experimental Inquiry. Journal of the ACM 8(3).
McCallum, A. & Nigam, K. (1998). A Comparison of Event Models for Naive Bayes Text Classification. AAAI-98 Workshop on Learning for Text Categorization.
Ng, A. Y. & Jordan, M. I. (2002). On Discriminative vs. Generative Classifiers: A Comparison of Logistic Regression and Naive Bayes. NeurIPS 14.
Hastie, T., Tibshirani, R. & Friedman, J. (2009). The Elements of Statistical Learning (2nd ed.). Springer.
Jurafsky, D. & Martin, J. H. (2024). Speech and Language Processing (3rd ed. draft), Ch. 4: Naive Bayes and Sentiment Classification.
